title: Super change in wake of losses
full_text:
Investment products offered by fast-growing retail platforms could face fresh scrutiny on their returns and fees as Treasury looks to lock in super trustee accountability in the wake of the Shield and First Guardian failures.
The federal government’s latest consultation on proposed changes to the annual super performance test has received broad industry support for weighing up carve-outs for certain investments, including emerging or alternative asset classes and climate-related investments, following years of complaints that the current test encourages benchmark hugging.
“We have heard for some time that the performance test, as it is currently written, deters and discourages investment in areas like housing and energy, venture capital and start-ups,” Treasurer Jim Chalmers said on Friday.
“We don’t want the performance test to unintentionally discourage investment in some of the most important parts of our economy.” But the proposal to expand the test to super products offered on investment platforms such as HUB24 and Netwealth has split the industry, with the retail super lobby opposing the changes as industry funds push for greater scrutiny on the platform market. Externally managed products offered on platforms are still excluded, as are retirement products. The government is now considering whether these products should be included.
summary: The Australian government is proposing changes to the superannuation performance test to enhance accountability and encourage investment in alternative asset classes, amid industry debate on the inclusion of retail super products.
publication:
Chalmers is going for broke. Will we end up a poorer country?

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: Treasury is reviewing the super performance test following recent fund failures.
There is industry support for exemptions for certain investment types to promote economic growth.
The proposal has sparked division between retail super platforms and industry funds regarding accountability.
relevance: These proposed changes could impact investment strategies and compliance requirements for actuaries and financial services professionals managing superannuation funds.
